Q1: I tried to be pretty structured with my studying, usually carving out about 23 hours a week spread across different days. Being 28 and having other responsibilities, I had to be intentional about when I sat down to study. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often record myself reading notes and listen back to them during commutes or while doing chores. The routine wasn't perfect, but I made it work around my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I could stick to a consistent schedule, but other times deadlines or personal obligations threw everything off. I'd try to front-load studying when I knew a busy period was coming, but honestly, life as a 28-year-old student means flexibility is essential. I adapted constantly.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check on assignments or see if there were any announcements about upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd go in specifically to watch lecture videos or participate in discussions. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan my LMS sessions around when I could access wifi at the library or campus. That constraint meant I logged in with purpose rather than just browsing.
Q4: It became routine when I started going to the campus library at the same times each week specifically to access course materials. I'd block out those hours knowing I needed to download what I could and engage with discussions while I had connectivity. After a few weeks, it just became part of my weekly rhythm. The CBT sessions I attended also helped me build better habits around managing my anxiety about keeping up.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me because of my auditory learning style. I'd watch them, sometimes multiple times, and really absorb the content by listening carefully. I also found the discussion forums helpful because hearing different perspectives from peers helped concepts click. When I could engage with those 14 posts I made, I felt like I was actually part of a learning community.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped over reading-heavy PDFs or text-based resources that required a lot of screen time. Without consistent internet, downloading large documents was a hassle, and honestly, reading on screens isn't how I learn best anyway. I'd prioritize the audio and video content over written materials, which probably hurt my exam performance in hindsight.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could tell it directly connected to understanding the course material or would help with assignments. If I could listen to it or discuss it with peers, even better. I also valued anything that gave me feedback on whether I was on the right track. With my high anxiety about performance, knowing I was doing something productive really mattered to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given my time constraints and internet limitations. Required work always took priority because I knew it counted toward my grade. But when I did engage with optional content, it actually felt more enjoyable because there was less pressure. The irony is that the optional stuff sometimes helped me understand things better than the required readings.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos when I had wifi and watch them later at home. I recorded audio of myself summarizing concepts and played those recordings constantly. I also used physical textbooks from the library and handwritten notes extensively. My actual study time was way more than what the LMS could ever capture.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be listening to my recorded summaries while cooking dinner or reviewing handwritten notes before bed. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also had study sessions with a small group of classmates where we'd quiz each other verbally. Those peer interactions beyond the 11 the LMS tracked were some of my most valuable learning moments.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach because of my internet situation. I'd access a video lecture once, download it if possible, and then rewatch it multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content four or five times. It was the only way I could make my auditory learning style work with my connectivity constraints.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming, even though my overall stress was manageable. Around exam time especially, I struggled to focus because I was so worried about failing. The CBT sessions helped me work through those moments, but there were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage fully. My attendance dropping to about 74% reflects some of those harder stretches.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely impacted my LMS usage since I'm older and have more going on than traditional students. When work got busy or family needed me, the LMS was the first thing to get pushed aside. Fatigue was real too—after a long day, mustering the energy to engage with course content was tough. I tried to compensate by being really efficient when I did log in.
Q14: Honestly, there were times I'd scroll through the LMS or watch a video but my mind was somewhere else entirely. My anxiety would have me logged in, technically present, but mentally I was worrying about whether I understood anything or if I'd fail the exam. Those sessions felt hollow even though they showed up as activity. Engagement isn't just being there; it's being mentally present.
Q15: I definitely postponed some of the heavier reading assignments because they felt so daunting and didn't match my learning style. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and focus on audio content instead. My anxiety made it worse—sometimes avoiding something felt easier than facing the stress of struggling through it. That procrastination probably contributed to my exam struggles.
Q16: Deadlines were major drivers for me. I'd check the LMS more frequently as due dates approached and prioritize whatever was coming up next. My task completion rate of 92% shows I got things done, but often it was a last-minute push. The structure of having clear deadlines actually helped my anxiety because at least I knew what I needed to do and when.
Q17: Feedback had a significant impact on my engagement. When I got positive feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going and reduced my anxiety temporarily. But when feedback was critical or my exam scores came back low, it was demoralizing. The disconnect between my assignment scores around 78% and my exam score of 43% was genuinely surprising and confusing. I'm still processing what went wrong there.
Q18: The course structure with regular deadlines and discussion requirements kept me accountable, which I needed. However, the balance between different types of content didn't always work for me as an auditory learner. I appreciated having videos available, but wished there were more audio-focused resources. The structure gave me a framework, but I had to adapt it to my own learning needs.
Q19: Having video content made engagement easier for me since I could listen and learn effectively that way. The discussion forums were also accessible and helped me feel connected. What made it harder was the heavy reliance on written materials and the assumption that everyone has consistent internet access. The exam format also seemed to test things differently than the assignments prepared me for.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain and keeps checking materials without feeling confident enough to submit work. They might be reviewing, re-reading, second-guessing themselves. Or maybe they're trying to understand something that isn't clicking, so they keep going back to resources. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety in motion.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat, though my exam score doesn't reflect it. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in my activity logs. Someone could absolutely understand material well through methods the LMS can't track—textbooks, study groups, audio recordings like I used. Low LMS activity might just mean they found more effective ways to learn outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, which resources were accessed, and completion of tracked activities. But they completely miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they watched or just had it playing. They miss offline study, the quality of learning, and the emotional experience of being a student. My 150 minutes of video watching doesn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded content.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and caring about learning, not just going through the motions. It's showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, it meant listening to recordings repeatedly until concepts made sense, discussing ideas with peers, and pushing through even when my anxiety made it hard. Engagement is effort and intention, not just clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while still pushing forward, or how the CBT sessions helped me develop resilience to keep going. It wouldn't capture my offline study habits, the peer conversations outside the platform, or how surprised and disappointed I was when my exam score didn't reflect my effort. It definitely wouldn't show that I'm a 28-year-old balancing multiple life responsibilities while trying to succeed academically.
